sources.notes: Samuel Hooker Jr. biography; confirms Plymouth birth, Hartford merchant activity

landmarks.landmark_blurb: His father lived here, directly opposite the Giles Hooker house on Main Street.

narrative_blocks.header: Born in Plymouth in his father's transition year
narrative_blocks.body: He was born in Plymouth, Massachusetts on May 29, 1661 — where his father Rev. Samuel Hooker had been preaching for several years. The family moved to Farmington that same year, where his father succeeded his brother-in-law Roger Newton as minister.

narrative_blocks.header: His house stood south of the burial ground
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Hooker II had a fine residence at Farmington, next south of the old burial ground — later known as the Mix house — and ran a store for mercantile business in Hartford. He was one of the first of the Farmington Hookers to move from ministry into trade.

narrative_blocks.header: He married a wealthy sea captain's daughter
narrative_blocks.body: In 1687 Samuel Hooker married Mehitabel Hamlin of Middletown, daughter of Capt. Giles Hamlin, a noted and wealthy sea captain and merchant. Hooker kept a store and traded out of Hartford while living at Farmington.

career.notes: Mercantile business. Store and fine residence at Farmington.

education.notes: B.A. Harvard; later a fellow of the college. (Julius Gay, Farmington Historical Pamphlets)

burial.plot_notes: Ancient Burying Ground, Hartford CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

sources.notes: Samuel Hooker Jr. biography; confirms Plymouth birth, Hartford merchant activity

landmarks.landmark_blurb: His father lived here, directly opposite the Giles Hooker house on Main Street.

narrative_blocks.header: Born in Plymouth in his father's transition year
narrative_blocks.body: He was born in Plymouth, Massachusetts on May 29, 1661 — where his father Rev. Samuel Hooker had been preaching for several years. The family moved to Farmington that same year, where his father succeeded his brother-in-law Roger Newton as minister.

narrative_blocks.header: His house stood south of the burial ground
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Hooker II had a fine residence at Farmington, next south of the old burial ground — later known as the Mix house — and ran a store for mercantile business in Hartford. He was one of the first of the Farmington Hookers to move from ministry into trade.

narrative_blocks.header: He married a wealthy sea captain's daughter
narrative_blocks.body: In 1687 Samuel Hooker married Mehitabel Hamlin of Middletown, daughter of Capt. Giles Hamlin, a noted and wealthy sea captain and merchant. Hooker kept a store and traded out of Hartford while living at Farmington.

career.notes: Mercantile business. Store and fine residence at Farmington.

education.notes: B.A. Harvard; later a fellow of the college. (Julius Gay, Farmington Historical Pamphlets)

burial.plot_notes: Ancient Burying Ground, Hartford CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of a sea captain
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Capt. Giles Hamlin was a noted and wealthy sea captain and merchant of Middletown, Connecticut. Her mother Esther Crow was the daughter of John Crow, merchant of Hartford — placing Mehitabel at the intersection of two of colonial Connecticut's most prominent merchant families.

bio.bio_blurb: Daughter of Middletown sea captain Giles Hamlin

burial.plot_notes: Ancient Burying Ground, Hartford CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.
Gravestone gives age 98. EH gives age 99.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: His father lived here, directly opposite the Giles Hooker house on Main Street.

narrative_blocks.header: He heard his brother died
narrative_blocks.body: On hearing that his younger brother Giles had died at age 96, Samuel Hooker "fell back in his chair and expired" in Kensington on March 1, 1787, age 98. Their sister later wrote: "her brother Samuel died in 1787, ae. 99 — a long lived race." Two brothers, together accounting for nearly 195 years of life, died within ten days.

narrative_blocks.header: His wife was a granddaughter of the Governor
narrative_blocks.body: Mercy Leete, whom he married in 1711, was the granddaughter of Governor William Leete of Connecticut Colony. His uncle James Hooker had also married a Leete granddaughter — Mary Leete, Mercy's first cousin through the same Governor. Two generations of the Hooker family married into the Leete gubernatorial line.

cross_connections.display_label: First cousins — both Leete granddaughters who married Hooker men.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of Governor William Leete
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Andrew Leete was the son of Governor William Leete, who governed both New Haven Colony and the Colony of Connecticut — the only person ever to hold both offices. Her first cousin Mary Leete (through the Governor's son William Jr.) also married a Hooker man in the same generation.

bio.notes: Also given as "Mary" — EH gives "Mercy (Mary) Leete"

parents.mother_research_notes: Mary Wyllys — second wife of Rev. Joseph Elliott. The Wyllys family were prominent Connecticut magistrates.

narrative_blocks.header: Her husband Moses lived to 96
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Hooker married Moses Gilbert in February 1734 and bore him six children in Berlin, Connecticut. Her son Ebenezer was killed in the Revolutionary Army on February 15, 1776, at age 34. Ebenezer's own son Sylvanus later died in the same army.

cross_connections.display_label: Niece by brother Samuel Jr. who married her son Hooker.
Her daughter Sarah married my son Hooker Gilbert in 1807.

sources.notes: Entry 118 — James Hurlbut. Lists Abigail Hart as first wife, not Mehitable Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Her recorded marriage belonged to another woman
narrative_blocks.body: The 1909 genealogy married her to Dr. James Hurlbut, but the Hurlbut Family Genealogy proves his wife was Abigail Hart — the error stood in the family record for a century. Mehitable’s own life after 1722 remains unrecorded.

cross_connections.display_label: Served in his Troop of horses, Fort William Henry alarm 1757.
Nephew killed June 1757; uncle enlisted that August.
Fellow Hooker descendant in same 1757 Fort William Henry alarm.
Sister Elizabeth's son who married his daughter Sarah 1807.
His son Hooker Gilbert married my daughter Sarah in 1807.

military_service.unit: Maj. Elizur Talcott of Glastonbury — Troop of horses
military_service.notes: At age 31 enlisted in Maj. Elizur Talcott of Glastonbury's Troop of horses for service in the alarm for the relief of Fort William Henry and parts adjacent, August 1757 — 14 days of service. Enlisted approximately two months after his nephew Martin Hooker (H00410) was killed June 10, 1757 in the same war. The connection to Maj. Elizur Talcott (T00459, gen 3 from John Talcott) is a Hooker↔Talcott cross-tree military link.

military_service.unit: Capt. Nathaniel Chapman's Company, Lt-Col Joseph Baldwin's Regiment, Col. Mead's drafted men
military_service.notes: Served under Capt. Nathaniel Chapman in Lt-Col Joseph Baldwin's Regiment at Fishkill, NY. DAR Ancestor #A057686 (Connecticut, Private). Source: Record of Service of Connecticut Men in the War of the Revolution (1889), p. 522.

narrative_blocks.header: Veteran of two colonial wars
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel served as Private in two of the colonial era's military campaigns twenty years apart. In August 1757 at age 31 he joined Maj. Elizur Talcott's Troop of horses for fourteen days during the Fort William Henry alarm. Twenty years later at age 51 he served again from December 1777 to March 1778 in Capt. Nathaniel Chapman's Company at Fishkill NY — earning DAR ancestor recognition #A057686.

narrative_blocks.header: Enlisted weeks after his nephew Martin's death
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel's nephew Martin Hooker — son of Samuel's brother Andrew — was killed in the same war on June 10, 1757 at the deadly sentry post documented in Edward Hooker's 1909 genealogy. About two months later in August 1757, Samuel Hooker Jr enlisted at age 31 in Maj. Elizur Talcott's Troop of horses for the Fort William Henry alarm. Whether the enlistment was motivated by news of Martin's death is undocumented but the timing is suggestive.

burial.plot_notes: Ledge Cemetery, Berlin, Hartford County, Connecticut. 41.62805, -72.76927

narrative_blocks.header: Thomas died young
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas died young; the year was not recorded.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: His father lived here, directly opposite the Giles Hooker house on Main Street.

narrative_blocks.header: His death at 96 killed his brother Samuel
narrative_blocks.body: On February 19, 1787, Giles Hooker died in Farmington at age 96. When the news reached his older brother Samuel — then age 98 in Kensington — Samuel "fell back in his chair and expired" on March 1, 1787. Two brothers, together accounting for nearly 195 years of life, died within ten days of each other.

narrative_blocks.header: The well stone on Main Street
narrative_blocks.body: He and Martha lived on the George Cowles place on Main Street, Farmington. In Edward Hooker's time (1909), a flat stone in the sidewalk just below the brow of the hill still covered the old well of the Giles Hooker house. Directly opposite, his father Rev. Samuel Hooker had lived in the Mix House — just south of the old burying ground.

narrative_blocks.header: Her grandfather was Secretary of State
narrative_blocks.body: Her maternal grandfather was Hon. John Allyn (Allen), Secretary of State for the Colony of Connecticut. Her father, Capt. Aaron Cooke IV, served as Hartford's deputy to the Connecticut General Assembly for fifteen sessions and chaired the committee to distribute the new laws in 1702.

cross_connections.display_label: Her second cousin Abigail married her husband's younger brother James.

narrative_blocks.header: Married widow Hannah Wells at fifty-seven
narrative_blocks.body: He married widow Hannah Wells at age 57 in a late marriage with no issue. Edward Hooker gives no further entry on him beyond his marriage and death.

cross_connections.display_label: First cousin John registered his Sue Squa deed

narrative_blocks.header: Four wives, five children — only one survived
narrative_blocks.body: He married four times across his 88 years. Of the five children produced across three of his four marriages, only one survived past childhood — and that son died unmarried at 27.

narrative_blocks.header: Sue Squa deed grantee on his cousin's land
narrative_blocks.body: On June 13, 1733 he received about three-quarters of an acre at Indian Neck, Farmington as part of the Sue Squa deed. His first cousin John Hooker — Hon. John Hooker, Probate Judge — served as witness, certifying official, and registrar of the same transaction.

bio.notes: EH gives "Woodward"; FindAGrave gives "Woodford" — same person, variant spelling
EH gives only "Esther, born 1705" — no surname recorded

cross_connections.display_label: Esther Hooker Stiles was the great-grandmother of Horace Stiles who.
Esther Hooker Stiles was the great-grandmother of Horace Stiles who.

narrative_blocks.header: Second wife of Rev. Isaac Stiles
narrative_blocks.body: She married Rev. Isaac Stiles of North Haven as his second wife. His first wife Keziah Taylor — daughter of the great Puritan poet Rev. Edward Taylor — had died in childbirth on December 4, 1727.

narrative_blocks.header: Raised Ezra Stiles, future Yale president
narrative_blocks.body: She raised her stepson Ezra Stiles from infancy after his birth mother Keziah Taylor's death. He became President of Yale (1778-1795) and one of the most important intellectuals of the American Founding.

narrative_blocks.header: Ten children with Stiles between 1729 and 1750
narrative_blocks.body: She and Isaac Stiles had ten children of their own between 1729 and 1750. Only Ezra was Keziah's son from the first marriage; all others were Esther's, carrying the Stiles-Hooker line forward through North Haven.

narrative_blocks.header: Her name traveled five generations as Esther Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: The name 'Esther Hooker' became a heritable token across five generations of Stiles women — from her (1702) to her granddaughter (1760), and onward through Alice Stiles Mills (1845). Her name carried farther than her lineage.

I00126.gravestone_inscription: This monument is erected to the memory of the Rev. Isaac Stiles, A.M., who was born in Windsor, July 30th, 1697, received a liberal education at Yale College; ordained to the pastoral office in the church of North Haven, November 11th, 1724, where he served in the ministry 36 years, and died May 14th, 1760, aged 63. ...he preached the gospel with fervor and fidelity. A friend to pure and undefiled religion, with a charitable benevolence to all mankind. Mors Mihi vita Est.

cross_connections.display_label: was father of his first wife, Keziah

narrative_blocks.header: Thirty-six years in North Haven’s pulpit
narrative_blocks.body: Yale 1722, Stiles held the North Haven pastorate for thirty-six years and raised — by his first wife — Ezra Stiles, the Enlightenment polymath who became Yale’s seventh president. His Hooker-descended children came by his second wife, Esther Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: He preached against his own melon thief
narrative_blocks.body: Spotting a parishioner stealing his melons during the Sunday intermission, Stiles named him from the afternoon pulpit: “I saw you, John Johnson, thou son of Belial… enter my garden and steal my melons.” His son Ezra called it “rather close preaching, but characteristic of the times.”

narrative_blocks.header: His first wife was the poet’s daughter
narrative_blocks.body: Keziah Taylor, daughter of the metaphysical poet Rev. Edward Taylor of Westfield, died in childbed in December 1727 — twenty-five years old, two years married. Within the year Stiles remarried, to Esther Hooker of Farmington, who bore his ten North Haven children.

narrative_blocks.header: First of his blood liberally educated
narrative_blocks.body: Yale 1722 — “the first of the name and blood that had a liberal education in America” — he published “A Looking-glass for Changlings” against political turncoats in 1743. His son Ezra became president of the college; his epitaph fills a monument at North Haven.

military_service.notes: Per The Stiles Family in America (BIB008) entry #230: served in the Revolution and was at Horse Neck from May 1781 to March 1782. Horse Neck (Greenwich CT) was an active Revolutionary War outpost under occasional British raid throughout the war.
Served in the Revolution at Horse Neck (now Greenwich CT, on the western frontier facing British-held New York), from May 1781 to March 1782.

narrative_blocks.header: Inherited mansion, lost all endorsing for friend
narrative_blocks.body: Ashbel Stiles inherited the family mansion and a very comfortable property from his father in North Haven Connecticut. But in an evil hour he endorsed for a friend and lost his all. Soon after he removed to Windsor Connecticut, and thence to Norwich Massachusetts — now Huntington — near his daughter Ruth Stiles Ellis.

narrative_blocks.header: Hannah died Norwich one month before Ashbel did
narrative_blocks.body: After fifty-one years of marriage, Ashbel and his cousin-wife Hannah Stiles both died at Norwich (now Huntington) Massachusetts in autumn 1810 — both at age seventy-five. Hannah went first in September; Ashbel followed in October, approximately one month later.

narrative_blocks.header: Son thrown from a horse, killed in Jamaica
narrative_blocks.body: His son Benjamin Stiles died at Jamaica in the West Indies from injuries sustained being thrown from a horse. The Stiles Family in America entry 230 records the death without further circumstance — a son lost an ocean from home to a single fall.

narrative_blocks.header: Horse Neck garrison 1781 after financial ruin
narrative_blocks.body: Forty-six at enlistment, he came to Revolutionary militia service well after most of his cohort. Financial ruin from endorsing for a friend had already emptied the family mansion he had inherited from his father.

bio.notes: In later years signed her name "Mabel"

political_offices.title: Town Clerk
political_offices.notes: Re-elected every year except 1757 until his death — 36 years of service

education.notes: — Yale 1761. Father of Capt. William Coit "The Great Red Dragon" (H00248). Revolutionary War figure.

career.role: Justice of the peace and county court clerk

narrative_blocks.header: Town clerk for thirty-seven years
narrative_blocks.body: Justice of the peace and New London’s town clerk from 1736 until his death, Coit also served as overseer of the Groton Pequots from 1741 and of the Mohegan from 1769. His second wife was Mehitable Hooker of Farmington.

H00248.prominent_name_reason: Capt. William Coit — Yale 1761; "The Great Red Dragon" (wore scarlet cloak); commanded schooner Harrison, one of first vessels of the Continental Navy; present at Battle of Bunker Hill; claimed first American to turn British flag upside down; commander of "Oliver Cromwell" (1776); captured by Benedict Arnold 1781; DAR Patriot A023936.

sources.short: CAPTAIN WILLIAM COIT by P.H. Woodward, Connecticut Historical Society, 1879

notable.notable_blurb: “The Great Red Dragon” of the Revolution

military_service.unit: 4th Company, 6th Connecticut Regiment; schooner Harrison; ship Oliver Cromwell
military_service.notes: Covered the retreat at Bunker Hill; commanded the Harrison from Oct 1775 under Washington’s written instructions; later the privateer America; captured when Arnold burned New London, Sept 6, 1781

bio.title: Captain
bio.notes: Known for wearing a scarlet cloak

narrative_blocks.header: Captain of one of America's first warships
narrative_blocks.body: In late 1775 he commanded the schooner Harrison — one of the first vessels commissioned by Congress under General Washington. He intercepted two British supply ships and brought them into Plymouth harbor.

narrative_blocks.header: Made British prisoners stand on Plymouth Rock
narrative_blocks.body: He landed the captured British prisoners on Plymouth Rock, where they 'gave three cheers, and wished success to American arms,' per the Pennsylvania Journal of 1775. The act inverted Plymouth's founding myth: now British prisoners stood on the rock.

narrative_blocks.header: The Great Red Dragon — scarlet cloak
narrative_blocks.body: He was tall, portly, soldierly in bearing, frank, jovial, somewhat eccentric, and very liberal. He wore a scarlet cloak that earned him the nickname 'The Great Red Dragon.'

narrative_blocks.header: First American to invert the British flag
narrative_blocks.body: He claimed to be 'the first man in the States who turned his majesty's bunting upside down' when he captured British vessels in late 1775. The symbolic act came early in the naval war.

narrative_blocks.header: “Cohorns that Noah had in the Ark”
narrative_blocks.body: His November 1775 letter savaged his own schooner: cannon “brought by the Lords Say and Seal” in 1635, swivels “never fired since” 1620, a deck that broke through over the magazine, smoke that “serves for bedding, victuals, drink and choking.” Firing both guns of a side, he wrote, would split her “from gunwale to her keelson.”

burial.plot_notes: No stone survives. Well-attested tradition places the grave at the south side of Rev. Samuel Whitman's marked grave. "Mr. Whitman like Mr. Hooker, died while the minister of the town, and tradition says he was buried by the side of Mr. Hooker."

sources.notes: John Winthrop Jr., Alchemy and the Creation of New England Culture, 1606-1676 (Homer Babbidge Prize)

narrative_blocks.header: He inherited his father's library — and his calling
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker's will gave his library to John on condition that John pursue ministry; if John faltered, the entire library and manuscripts went to Samuel. John did not become a minister. Samuel received his father's books — and then dedicated his own life to the ministry his father had hoped for.

narrative_blocks.header: Prosecutor in the Hartford witch trials
narrative_blocks.body: In the 1660s he formed a prosecutorial tribunal with Rev. Samuel Stone of Hartford and Rev. Joseph Haynes of Wethersfield. Connecticut tried witches more aggressively than Massachusetts in this decade — three decades before the Salem trials.

narrative_blocks.header: Ordered to King Philip's War, then quietly replaced
narrative_blocks.body: On March 9, 1675-6, the Council ordered Hooker to prepare to march into King Philip's War with Major Treat's army toward Hadley and Northampton. Two days later, the Council quietly replaced him with Rev. Israel Chauncy. No reason was recorded — only the substitution.

narrative_blocks.header: He persuaded Farmington to found a free school
narrative_blocks.body: In December 1685, the town of Farmington voted to give £30 for a schoolmaster for one year — and declared it a free school for the town, almost certainly at Hooker's instigation. It was among the earliest free public schools in colonial Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: The 'Fervent Hooker,' who preached from the heart
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Pitkin described him as 'an excellent preacher, his composition good, his address pathetic, warm and engaging.' Hooker told a friend he had three things to do with his sermons before delivering them: write them, commit them to memory, and get them into his heart.

cross_connections.display_label: Five Hooker generations on the same Farmington ground.
Farmington's first pastor, whom Hooker succeeded
His grandfather Tobie Willet sailed with John Talcott on the Lion.
Her husband's Prudden line connects to his wife Mary Willet's family
was his father-in-law; First Mayor of New York City, 1665

quotes.text: Thus we have to this day among us our dead Hooker, yet living in his worthy son Samuel Hooker, an able, faithful, useful minister at Farmington, in the Colony of Connecticut.

education.notes: B.A. 1653. Entered Harvard 1651. Chosen Fellow of the College November 27, 1654.

burial.plot_notes: Near center of burying ground, near East Norwalk railway station

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of New York City's first mayor
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Capt. Thomas Willet was appointed the first Mayor of New York City in 1665, when the English renamed the Dutch colony. He had previously succeeded Capt. Miles Standish as commander of Plymouth's militia — one of the most storied military commands in early New England. Mary carried this distinguished lineage to Farmington when she married Rev. Samuel Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Three generations of Willet women known for beauty
narrative_blocks.body: Mary's mother was described as possessing 'beauty and majesty almost divine.' A portrait of Mary's daughter Mary Hooker Pierpont survives, and from it contemporaries formed 'the opinion that she was a woman of uncommon beauty.' Her daughter Sarah was 'accounted the most accomplished lady that had ever come to Norwalk.'

narrative_blocks.header: Remarried at 66 — outlived two minister husbands
narrative_blocks.body: After Samuel Hooker's death in 1697, Mary married Reverend Thomas Buckingham of Saybrook at age 66. When he died in 1709 she moved to Norwalk to live with her son-in-law Reverend Stephen Buckingham, where she remained until her death in 1712 at age 74.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her daughter married father and son
narrative_blocks.body: After Rev. Samuel Hooker died, Mary Willet married Rev. Thomas Buckingham of Saybrook in 1703. Her daughter Sarah was the wife of his son, Rev. Stephen Buckingham, so a mother and daughter had married a father and his son.

cross_connections.display_label: Her grandfather Tobie Willet sailed to New England with John Talcott.
Her father Thomas Willet's widow's first husband was ancestor of H00153's husband

burial.plot_notes: Exact location unknown; earliest surviving identifiable gravestone in cemetery dates to 1648 Memorial plaque text: "Thomas Hooker 1586-1647. A leader of the founders in this commonwealth. A preacher of persuasive power. A statesman who based all civil authority on the free consent of the people."

marriages.officiant: Rev. Robert Chaloner, D.D., rector of Amersham (Susanna's aunt's husband)

sources.full: J.R. Hutchinson, 'The Rev. Thomas Hooker—An Alternative Descent,' NY G&B Record 48 (1917), p. 393
Josephine C. Frost, NY G&B Record 64 (Jan 1933), p. 2

narrative_blocks.header: Led 100 settlers from Cambridge to found Hartford
narrative_blocks.body: In the summer of 1636, Hooker led a company of one hundred men, women, and children from Newtown (Cambridge) to found Hartford — a hundred miles through wilderness with no path, guided only by a compass. They drove 160 cattle before them, cleared thickets with axes, built causeways from felled trees, rested in shady groves, and ate wild strawberries. They made the journey in a fortnight.

narrative_blocks.header: His 1638 sermon framed Connecticut's constitution
narrative_blocks.body: On May 31, 1638, preaching before the General Court assembled to frame Connecticut's constitution, Hooker declared: "The foundation of authority is laid in the free consent of the people." The Fundamental Orders of Connecticut, adopted January 14, 1639 — often called the world's first written democratic constitution — grew from this sermon.

narrative_blocks.header: Called the light of the western churches
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker was universally regarded by his contemporaries as the foremost preacher in New England — Cotton Mather called him "the light of the western churches."

narrative_blocks.header: He moderated the Hutchinson trial, then left
narrative_blocks.body: In 1637, Hooker served as one of two moderators over the three-week inquiry into Anne Hutchinson's antinomian doctrines — which culminated in her excommunication and banishment from Massachusetts Bay. Having completed his role, Hooker returned to Hartford before the trial concluded.

narrative_blocks.header: No portrait exists — only his descendants' faces
narrative_blocks.body: There is no known portrait of Thomas Hooker. The statue that stands before the Old State House in Hartford — near the site of the First Meeting House — was sculpted by comparing the likenesses of his descendants.

cross_connections.display_label: was Hooker's deacon at Cambridge; followed to Hartford
neighbored Hooker's Cambridge circle in Hertfordshire
co-moderated the 1637 Hutchinson trial
co-founded Hartford with Hooker
held the Cambridge lot adjoining Hooker's
married Hannah Holyoke, linking both founding families
co-wrote the Fundamental Orders; first Connecticut governor
served as the colony’s treasurer from 1641 until his death

quotes.text: His genius would still haunt all the pulpits. He had great popularity and influence, especially with young ministers, to whom he was an oracle, and their principle library.

Having gone on, with much strength of voice and intention of spirit, about a quarter of an hour, he was at a stand and told the people, that God had deprived him of both his strength and matter, and so went forth and about half an hour after, returned again, and went on to very good purpose about tw
I giue vnto my sonne Samuell, in case the whole library come not to him... the summe of seuenty pounds.
Brother, I am going to receive mercy!
quotes.attribution: Thomas Hooker, deathbed, July 7, 1647 (Cotton Mather, Magnalia Christi Americana)

career.notes: Cared for Mrs. Joanna Drake; 'had his diet and lodging' in Drake household
Silenced by Bishop Laud for nonconformity
After silencing; John Eliot was assistant

education.notes: B.A. 1608; M.A. 1611; Fellow 1611-c.1618 — Entered divinity course; left before B.D.

narrative_blocks.header: They met in the same household in Surrey
narrative_blocks.body: Susanna Garbrand was working as a waiting-gentlewoman to Mrs. Joanna Drake at Esher, Surrey, when Thomas Hooker arrived in 1620 as curate of Esher church — "having his diet and lodging" in the Drake household. They married at Amersham on April 3, 1621, the ceremony performed by Susanna's own uncle by marriage, Rev. Robert Chaloner.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of Oxford's Reformation bookseller
narrative_blocks.body: Susanna descended from the Garbrands, a Calvinist family who fled the Low Countries for England. Her grandfather Garbrand Harks became the foremost Oxford bookseller of the Reformation and official stationer to the University, a post the family held for three generations.

narrative_blocks.header: Crossed on the Griffin in the great migration
narrative_blocks.body: In 1633 Susanna sailed to New England aboard the Griffin with her husband and children, part of the Puritan migration that brought some twenty thousand settlers before 1640. The family settled first at Cambridge, then helped found Hartford in 1636.

narrative_blocks.header: Remarried Elder William Goodwin after Hooker's death
narrative_blocks.body: After Thomas Hooker died in 1647, Susanna married Elder William Goodwin, a leading Hartford churchman. In 1654 a Hartford court punished Walter Gray for slandering the couple with a charge of adultery.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived Hooker by nearly thirty years
narrative_blocks.body: Susanna outlived Thomas Hooker by almost thirty years, dying at Farmington in 1676 at about eighty-three. Through her eight children the Hooker line descended to figures including the financier J.P. Morgan and President William Howard Taft.

burial.plot_notes: Marker: rough stone with rude inscription. Marker present.

notable.notable_blurb: First Mayor of New York City, 1665

narrative_blocks.header: From Plymouth Pilgrim to New York's first mayor
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Willet was appointed the first Mayor of New York City by Governor Richard Nicolls in 1665, shortly after the English took the city from the Dutch. He had joined the English expedition against New Amsterdam the previous year alongside Governor Winthrop of Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Succeeded Miles Standish as Plymouth's military captain
narrative_blocks.body: A trusted Plymouth Colony leader, Willet succeeded the legendary Miles Standish as Captain of the Plymouth military company in 1648. He had arrived in Plymouth on the ship Lion in 1632, associated with the Leyden congregation.

narrative_blocks.header: He bought Attleboro from Massasoit's son
narrative_blocks.body: As a Plymouth trader, Willett negotiated the Rehoboth North Purchase, acquiring the lands that became Attleboro and North Attleboro. He bought them from Wamsutta, a son of the Wampanoag sachem Massasoit.

narrative_blocks.header: He brokered New Amsterdam's surrender
narrative_blocks.body: In 1664 Willett joined the English expedition under Richard Nicolls and helped arrange the peaceful surrender of Dutch New Amsterdam. When the city was renamed New York, he became its first mayor the next year.

cross_connections.display_label: sailed with Willet aboard the Lion in 1632.
His second wife Joanna Prudden was ancestral to her husband's family
married his daughter Mary in 1658
was Boston's first mayor, as Willett was New York's.

career.role: Commander of trading post on Penobscot
Captain of military company of Plymouth

career.event: Joined English expedition against New Amsterdam with Gov. Winthrop of Connecticut

career.role: First Mayor of New York City
career.notes: Proclaimed; resigned as Assistant of Plymouth Colony

career.event: Returned to Massachusetts; founded town of Swansea

military_service.war: Anglo-Dutch War / Conquest of New Amsterdam
military_service.role: Joined English expedition against New Amsterdam with Governor Winthrop of Connecticut
military_service.notes: After the conquest he became first Mayor of New York City 1665
Succeeded Miles Standish as Captain of Plymouth military company in 1648

bio.title: Captain
bio.bio_blurb: First mayor of New York City; Plymouth militia captain
Wife of New York's first mayor; daughter of Major John Brown

burial.plot_notes: Ancient Little Neck Cemetery (Bullock's Cove), East Providence RI. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of Plymouth's Major John Brown
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Browne was a daughter of Major John Brown, a leading citizen of Plymouth Colony. In 1636 she married the trader Thomas Willett, who would become New York's first mayor.

narrative_blocks.header: She bore fourteen children across two decades
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Browne and Thomas Willett had fourteen children over more than twenty years at Plymouth. She died early in 1670, and her husband remarried the following year.

burialCemetery.name: Ancient Burying Ground
burialCemetery.notes: Adjacent to Center Church. Earliest surviving identifiable gravestone dates to 1648. Resting place of many Hartford founders including Rev. Thomas Hooker (exact location unknown).

INST002.name: Harvard College
INST002.notes: Rev. Thomas Shepard (I00002) was instrumental in founding Harvard and placing it at Cambridge. Rev. Samuel Shepard (H00008) graduated 1658; Samuel Shepard grandson (H00038) graduated 1685; Col. Roger Newton (H00044) also a graduate.
